unit.subject: English
unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 3! In this unit you will learn all about time and daily life. You will name the twelve months of the year and put them in order. You will talk about your daily routine — what you do at each hour of the day. You will learn to talk about the past (what already happened), the present (what happens every day), and the future (what will happen soon). By the end, you will be able to make your own calendar, just like Sarah does in this unit's story, “The Calendar on the Wall”.
unit.learningPath:
Start with the Vocabulary file. Learn a few months and time words each day.
Work through the seven short lessons in the Grammar file, one lesson a day.
Read the Story file, “The Calendar on the Wall”, and answer the questions.
Work through the four parts below: Reading & Listening → Speaking → Writing → Activities.
Finish with the Self-Assessment to see what you have learned.

visual.alt: A child planning daily activities with a clock and calendar

rubrics.level4: Uses target words accurately and naturally

rubrics.level1: Capital letters and punctuation need support

outcomes.learningOutcome: Name and spell the twelve months of the year, in order.
Talk about your daily routine using the hours of the day.
Use the Present Simple to talk about what you do every day.
Use the Past Simple and irregular verbs to talk about what already happened.
Talk about the future and about what will happen soon.
Compare things with -er and -est (comparatives and superlatives).
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Independent writing, presentation or project evidence

outcomes.learningOutcome: Ask and answer Yes/No and Wh- questions about time.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Independent writing, presentation or project evidence

outcomes.learningOutcome: Give simple instructions using imperatives (Prepare your calendar. Plan your day.).
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Independent writing, presentation or project evidence

readings.title: My Day, Hour by Hour
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript: My name is Ishmael. I have a busy day. At 6 o’clock, I wake up and pray. At 7, I eat breakfast with my family. At 8, I walk to school. We study for many hours. At 1 o’clock, we eat lunch. At 4, I go home and help my mother. In the evening, I read a book. Every day is a little different, but I always try to plan my time.

readings.title: The Twelve Months
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript: A year has twelve months. The first month is January, and the last is December. Some months are hot and some are cool. Some months have rain and some are dry. In our school, the year begins in January and ends in November. Families use a calendar to remember special days, such as Eid, birthdays, and the days to plant and pick their crops.

readings.title: Making a Calendar
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript: “I made a calendar for the whole year. In June, we have school games. In August, we go on vacation to visit my grandma. In October, we pick mangoes. I marked every special month!”

readings.title: The Months Song
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Sing along, and count the months on your fingers:
“January, February, March and April, May and June, July, August, September, October, November, December — twelve months make one year!”

readings.title: The Calendar on the Wall
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
One bright Monday morning, Sarah sat in class and looked at the big calendar on the wall. Her teacher had just asked a question.
“When does the school year begin?”
“In January,” said Sarah.
“Good,” said the teacher. “And when does it end?”
“In November,” said another student.
“Correct. Now let us each make a calendar that shows what we do each month.”
Sarah liked the idea. She wrote her name at the top of her paper and drew a square for each month: January, February, March, and so on.
Then she thought about the future.
“In June, we have school games,” she wrote. “In August, we go on vacation to visit Grandma.”
That night, at home, she showed the calendar to her grandmother.
“In October, we pick mangoes,” said Grandma, smiling. “And in December, the weather is hot.”
“What happened in the old days, Grandma?” asked Sarah.
“Oh, a lot,” she said. “A century ago, people did not have printed calendars. They watched the sky, the moon, and the trees to know the time of year.”
Sarah’s eyes grew wide. “A hundred years ago?”
“Yes,” Grandma laughed. “A century sounds long, but it goes faster than you think.”
The next day at school, the teacher asked, “Did you prepare your calendar?”
“Yes,” said Sarah. “And I asked my Grandma about the past.”
“Great,” said the teacher. “Now add some details. What happens each hour of your day?”
Sarah wrote: “At 6 o’clock, I wake up. At 7, I eat breakfast. At 8, I walk to school. At 1, we eat lunch. At 4, I go home and help with the chores.”
Then she thought about the future again. “When I grow up,” she wrote, “I want to be a teacher. I will help students plan their day and prepare for the next century.”
Later in the week, the school had a quiz.
“What month comes sooner — April or May?” asked the teacher.
“April,” said Sarah. “It comes sooner.”
“And what will happen soon?” asked the teacher.
“Our vacation!” the class shouted together.
At the end of the week, parents visited the school. Sarah showed her calendar to her mother.
“Wow,” said her mother. “You even marked the days we plant maize.”
Sarah smiled. “I want to remember everything.”
Her mother added, “That is smart. The calendar helps us plan.”
On the last page of her book, Sarah wrote a short poem:
The months go fast, the hours too.
The future is coming, it’s all brand new.
Look at the past, remember it well,
Time is a story we all must tell.

grammar.title: Present Simple
grammar.explanation: We use the Present Simple to talk about things that happen again and again, and about facts that are always true. It is the “routine tense”. When we say “She marks the calendar every morning,” we mean she does it regularly. This helps us describe our daily life clearly.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Start with the subject: I, you, he, she, it, we, they.
Use the base verb (the verb with no “to”): mark, play, go.
For he, she, it, add s or es: “She marks the calendar.” “He watches TV.”
Negative: use do not / does not + base verb: “She does not play soccer.”
Question: start with Do / Does: “Does he go to school at 8 o’clock?”
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “She mark the calendar.”  ✗
Fix: For she, add the “s”: “She marks the calendar.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Think of the Present Simple as the routine tense. He, she and it never forget their “s”!
grammar.practice: Fill in the blank with the Present Simple form of the verb in brackets. | My brother ______ (play) games every weekend. | The cat ______ (chase) the mouse in the garden. | We ______ (not eat) junk food. | ______ (she / read) a book every night? | The train ______ (leave) at 9 o’clock.

grammar.title: Past Simple
grammar.explanation: We use the Past Simple to talk about things that already happened at a certain time in the past. It helps us tell stories and share what we did. When we say “We went on vacation in August,” we are talking about the past.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
For most verbs, add -ed to the base verb: play → played, visit → visited.
Some verbs are irregular and change fully: go → went, see → saw (see Lesson 3).
Negative: use did not (didn’t) + base verb: “I didn’t see the calendar.”
Question: start with Did + subject + base verb: “Did you see the calendar?”
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “She finish her homework yesterday.”  ✗
Fix: Add -ed for regular verbs: “She finished her homework yesterday.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: The Past Simple is a time machine. To travel back, add “-ed” to regular verbs — and learn the special irregular ones by heart.
grammar.practice: Fill in the blank with the Past Simple form of the verb in brackets. | Last year, we ______ (go) to the beach. | I ______ (see) a good film last weekend. | They ______ (play) in the park yesterday. | She ______ (visit) her friend last month.

grammar.title: Irregular Past Verbs
grammar.explanation: Most verbs add “-ed” in the past, but some special verbs change in their own way. These are irregular past verbs. We must learn them by heart, because they do not follow the “-ed” rule.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Change the whole verb to its special past form. Some common ones:
go → went:  “They went on vacation.”
see → saw:  “She saw the calendar.”
eat → ate:  “He ate his lunch quickly.”
have → had:  “We had a great time.”
take → took:  “I took my dog for a walk.”
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “They goed to the store.”  ✗
Fix: “go” is irregular, so it becomes went: “They went to the store.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Make flashcards: write the present verb on one side (go) and the past on the other (went). Practise with a friend or your AI tutor.
grammar.practice: Fill in the blank with the correct irregular past verb from the word bank: went, saw, ate, had, took. | Yesterday, I ______ my homework out of my bag. | They ______ to the park last week. | She ______ a delicious sandwich for lunch. | We ______ a great time at the wedding. | He ______ the film last night.

grammar.title: Comparatives and Superlatives
grammar.explanation: We use comparatives to compare two things (bigger, warmer). We use superlatives to talk about the best or the most of three or more things (the biggest, the warmest). This helps us describe and compare clearly.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Short adjectives — comparative adds -er, superlative adds -est: warm → warmer → the warmest.
Adjectives ending in -y — change y to i: happy → happier → the happiest.
Long adjectives — use more and the most: beautiful → more beautiful → the most beautiful.
Irregular: good → better → the best;  bad → worse → the worst.
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “He is more tall than his brother.”  ✗
Fix: “tall” is short, so add “-er”: “He is taller than his brother.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Think: “-er” is one step up (comparing two), and “-est” is the top of the steps (comparing many). “June is warmer than April; July is the warmest.”
grammar.practice: Fill in the blank with the correct form of the adjective in brackets. | My sister is ______ (smart) than I am. | This puzzle is the ______ (hard) one I have ever tried. | A cheetah is ______ (fast) than a lion. | This is the ______ (funny) film I have seen all year.

grammar.title: Yes/No and Wh- Questions
grammar.explanation: We ask questions to find out things. Yes/No questions can be answered with yes or no (“Did you miss the lesson?”). Wh- questions ask for more information and begin with who, what, when, where, why or how (“When is your vacation?”).
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Yes/No: Helping verb + subject + main verb?  “Did you miss the lesson?”
Helping verbs include: do, does, did, is, are, was, were, can, will.
Wh-: Wh- word + helping verb + subject + main verb?  “When did you go?”
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “Your vacation when is in September?”  ✗
Fix: Put the words in order — Wh- word first: “When is your vacation in September?”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Remember the pattern: for Yes/No questions, “Helping verb + subject + main verb?”. For Wh- questions, add the Wh- word at the very front.
grammar.practice: Choose the correct word to complete each question. | ______ (Do / Did) you enjoy the film? (it is over now) | ______ (What / Where) is your favourite book? | ______ (Can / Will) you help me with my project, please? | ______ (Who / When) is your best friend?

grammar.title: Imperatives (Giving Instructions) + Pronouns
grammar.explanation: Imperatives are sentences that give a command, an instruction or a request. They tell someone to do something. We use them a lot when we explain how to do something — like how to make a calendar.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Start with the base verb: Prepare your calendar. Plan your day.
No subject — we do not say “you”. Not “You clean your room”, just “Clean your room.”
Negative: add do not / don’t: “Don’t be late!”
Polite: add please: “Please listen carefully.”
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “Preparing your calendar.”  ✗
Fix: Use the base verb, not the -ing form: “Prepare your calendar.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Imperatives are action words. When you want someone to do something, just say the action — and remember, there is no “you” in an imperative.
grammar.practice: Fill in the blank with the correct imperative (base) form of the verb in brackets. | ______ your homework before dinner. (finish) | ______ the plants every day. (water) | ______ your shoes before you enter. (take off) | ______ the table for lunch. (set) | ______ your teacher when you have a question. (ask) | Lesson 7:  Pronouns | What it means and why it matters | Pronouns are small words we use instead of a noun, so we do not repeat the same name again and again. Instead of “Sophia is an artist. Sophia loves to paint,” we say “Sophia is an artist. She loves to paint.” This makes our sentences smoother. | How to form it | I — for yourself: “I like reading.” | he — one boy or man;  she — one girl or woman. | it — a thing, animal or idea: “It is raining.” | we — you and others;  they — more than one person or thing. | A common mistake | Mistake: “Her is my friend.”  ✗ | Fix: Use the subject pronoun at the start: “She is my friend.”  ✓ | Memory tip | First find the noun, then swap in the matching pronoun: a girl → she, a thing → it, a group → they. | Practice 7 | Choose the correct pronoun to complete each sentence. | ______ am going to the market. (I / They) | Samira is my best friend. ______ is very kind. (She / He) | The cat is sleeping. ______ looks so cute! (It / They) | Tom and I are friends. ______ like to play football. (We / He) | The girls are laughing. ______ are having fun. (They / She)

speaking.title: Speaking 1 — My Daily Routine
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Get ready to tell your AI tutor about your day. Use the hours:
“At ______ o’clock, I ______.”
“In the morning, I ______.”
“In the evening, I ______.”
Record yourself. Check: Did you say at least four things? Did you use the hours of the day? Did you speak clearly?
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise my daily routine and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 2 — Past, Present and Future
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Say one sentence for each time:
Past: “Last vacation, I ______.” (use a past verb, like went or visited)
Present: “Every day, I ______.”
Future: “In the future, I want to ______.”
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise past, present and future and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 3 — When Is It?
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Play with your AI tutor or your family:
One person names something, such as a birthday, Eid, or the school games.
The other person asks: “When is it?”
Answer with a month: “It is in ______.” Then swap turns.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise when is it? and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 4 — Vocabulary Expert Interview
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Choose six unit words. Ask and answer a clear question about each word using complete sentences.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise vocabulary expert interview and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 5 — Explain and Respond
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Explain one important unit idea, give a reason with because, and respond to two follow-up questions.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise explain and respond and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 6 — One-Minute Unit Talk
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Plan and record a one-minute talk using an opening, three connected details, and a closing sentence.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise one-minute unit talk and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

writing.title: Writing 1 — My Daily Routine (Information Report)
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Write four or five sentences about what you do each day. Use the hours and the Present Simple:
At 6 o’clock, I ______.
At 7, I ______.
At 8, I ______.
In the evening, I ______.
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 3 response uses complete connected sentences, precise unit words, and relevant details about my daily routine (information report).
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; used precise Unit vocabulary; organised connected ideas; checked capitals and punctuation; reread and improved my work
writing.support: Plan three ideas, use the word bank, and say each sentence before writing.
writing.extension: Add a stronger opening, two precise details, and a concluding sentence.

writing.title: Writing 2 — My Last Vacation (Summary)
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Write three sentences about your last vacation. Use the Past Simple. Answer: When did it happen? Where did you go? What did you do?
Example: “Last August, we went to visit my grandma. We picked mangoes and played games. It was a happy vacation.”
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 3 response uses complete connected sentences, precise unit words, and relevant details about my last vacation (summary).
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; used precise Unit vocabulary; organised connected ideas; checked capitals and punctuation; reread and improved my work
writing.support: Plan three ideas, use the word bank, and say each sentence before writing.
writing.extension: Add a stronger opening, two precise details, and a concluding sentence.

writing.title: Writing 3 — How to Make a Calendar (Instructions)
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Write three instructions that tell someone how to make a calendar. Start each one with a command word (an imperative). See Grammar Lesson 6.
Example: “First, draw twelve squares. Next, write one month in each square. Then, mark your special days.”
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 3 response uses complete connected sentences, precise unit words, and relevant details about how to make a calendar (instructions).
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; used precise Unit vocabulary; organised connected ideas; checked capitals and punctuation; reread and improved my work
writing.support: Plan three ideas, use the word bank, and say each sentence before writing.
writing.extension: Add a stronger opening, two precise details, and a concluding sentence.

writing.title: Writing 4 — Connected Paragraph
writing.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write a six-sentence paragraph about the unit. Begin with a topic sentence and join ideas with and, but or because.
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 3 response uses complete connected sentences, precise unit words, and relevant details about connected paragraph.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; used precise Unit vocabulary; organised connected ideas; checked capitals and punctuation; reread and improved my work
writing.support: Plan three ideas, use the word bank, and say each sentence before writing.
writing.extension: Add a stronger opening, two precise details, and a concluding sentence.

writing.title: Writing 5 — Information Text
writing.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write a heading and five accurate facts. Use precise unit vocabulary and check spelling and punctuation.
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 3 response uses complete connected sentences, precise unit words, and relevant details about information text.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; used precise Unit vocabulary; organised connected ideas; checked capitals and punctuation; reread and improved my work
writing.support: Plan three ideas, use the word bank, and say each sentence before writing.
writing.extension: Add a stronger opening, two precise details, and a concluding sentence.

writing.title: Writing 6 — Reflect and Improve
writing.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
writing.promptAndInstructions: Choose earlier writing, revise three sentences, add two useful details, and explain one improvement you made.
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 3 response uses complete connected sentences, precise unit words, and relevant details about reflect and improve.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; used precise Unit vocabulary; organised connected ideas; checked capitals and punctuation; reread and improved my work
writing.support: Plan three ideas, use the word bank, and say each sentence before writing.
writing.extension: Add a stronger opening, two precise details, and a concluding sentence.

activities.title: Activities 1 — Put the Months in Order
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Write these months in the correct order: May, January, March, April, February, June.
Answer: January, February, March, April, May, June.
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 2 — True or False (Time Facts)
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Write True or False. If it is false, write the correct answer.
There are 12 months in a year.
There are 60 minutes in one hour.
A century is 10 years.
September comes sooner than November.
Answers: 1. True   2. True   3. False (a century is 100 years)   4. True
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 3 — Past or Present?
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Choose the correct verb.
Every morning, she ______ (marks / marked) the calendar.
Last week, we ______ (go / went) on vacation.
They ______ (play / played) football yesterday.
He ______ (walks / walked) to school every day.
Answers: 1. marks   2. went   3. played   4. walks
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 4 — Fill in the Blanks
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Today's month is ______.
My favourite month is ______, because ______.
There are ______ months in a year.
In the future, I want to ______.
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 5 — Vocabulary Sort and Explain
activities.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
activities.instructionsAndItems: Sort twelve unit words into useful categories. Explain the rule for each category in a complete sentence.
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 6 — Reading Evidence Hunt
activities.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
activities.instructionsAndItems: Find six important details in the unit texts. Record the detail and explain what it helps the reader understand.
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

assignments.title: Time and Daily Life learning portfolio
assignments.instructions: Submit Writing 1 — My Daily Routine (Information Report) and a recording of Speaking 1 — My Daily Routine. Improve both pieces using the Grade 3 rubrics.
assignments.sourceFile: Derived from unit writing and speaking tasks

quizzes.question: What does 'January' mean?
quizzes.options: The fifth month of the year. | The first month of the year. | The fourth month of the year, when flowers bloom in many places. | The third month of the year.
quizzes.correctAnswer: The first month of the year.
quizzes.explanation: The school year begins in January.

quizzes.question: What does 'February' mean?
quizzes.options: The fifth month of the year. | The fourth month of the year, when flowers bloom in many places. | The second month of the year, usually with 28 days. | The sixth month of the year, often warm and sunny.
quizzes.correctAnswer: The second month of the year, usually with 28 days.

quizzes.question: What does 'March' mean?
quizzes.options: The fifth month of the year. | The seventh month of the year, often very warm. | The sixth month of the year, often warm and sunny. | The third month of the year.
quizzes.correctAnswer: The third month of the year.
quizzes.explanation: The rains often come in March.

quizzes.question: What does 'April' mean?
quizzes.options: The eighth month of the year. | The fourth month of the year, when flowers bloom in many places. | The seventh month of the year, often very warm. | The sixth month of the year, often warm and sunny.
quizzes.correctAnswer: The fourth month of the year, when flowers bloom in many places.
quizzes.explanation: We plant our garden in April.

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'The Twelve Months': The first month is _____, and the last is December.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: The first month is January, and the last is December.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'The Calendar on the Wall': She wrote her name at the top of her paper and drew a square for each month: January, _____, March, and so on.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: She wrote her name at the top of her paper and drew a square for each month: January, February, March, and so on.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'The Calendar on the Wall': “What month comes sooner — _____ or May?” asked the teacher.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: “What month comes sooner — April or May?” asked the teacher.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'The Calendar on the Wall': “In _____, we have school games,” she wrote.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: “In June, we have school games,” she wrote.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'Making a Calendar': In _____, we go on vacation to visit my grandma.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: In August, we go on vacation to visit my grandma.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'The Calendar on the Wall': “In _____, we pick mangoes,” said Grandma, smiling.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: “In October, we pick mangoes,” said Grandma, smiling.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

teacherNotes.note: Model new language aloud, check meaning before independent work, use mixed-response modes, and give specific feedback on complete sentences, text evidence and vocabulary precision.

💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you read the two passages with me and explain any hard words?”
“Can you play a listening game about the months of the year?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you help me practise talking about my daily routine?”
“Can you play the ‘When Is It?’ game with me?”
“Can you check that I used the past and future correctly?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you check if my sentences are correct?”
“Did I begin every month with a capital letter?”
“Can you help me make my instructions clear and in order?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you quiz me on the months in order?”
“How do I say and spell this month: ______?”
“Which months are hot and which months have rain where I live?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you play a months-of-the-year game with me?”
“What comes after October? What comes before September?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you help me use the word ‘century’ in a sentence?”
“What is the difference between an hour and a day?”
“Can you check my sentence about the future?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“What is the difference between ‘soon’ and ‘sooner’?”
“Can you give me two sentences using ‘sooner’?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you check my grammar answers?”
“Why do we say ‘she likes’ but ‘I like’?”
“Can you give me five more sentences to practise the Past Simple?”

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: The first month of the year. The school year begins in January.
The second month of the year, usually with 28 days. February is the shortest month.
The third month of the year. The rains often come in March.
The fourth month of the year, when flowers bloom in many places. We plant our garden in April.
The fifth month of the year. My cousin was born in May.
The sixth month of the year, often warm and sunny. In June, we have school games.
The seventh month of the year, often very warm. We go to visit family in July.
The eighth month of the year. In August, we go on vacation to visit Grandma.
The ninth month, when school often starts again. New books arrive in September.
The tenth month of the year. In October, we pick mangoes.

selfAssessment.statement: I can use the Past Simple to talk about what happened.

selfAssessment.statement: I can talk about the future and what will happen soon.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can compare things with -er and -est.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself
